---
title: "Group Assignment Causal Machine Learning"
authors: "Egor Trushkov, 6672874; Dominic Pöltl, 6618670"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_notebook:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
---

### 2.1 Literature Review

...

### 2.2 Identification of LATT/LATU

#### LATT

To identify the LATT we use the following standard IV assumptions:

(A1) Random Assignment of Z: $Z \perp (Y(1), Y(0), D(1), D(0))$

(A2) Exclusion Restriction: $Y(d, z) = Y(d)$ for all $d, z$

This also implies:

\$\$

Y(D(1)) = Y(1)D(1) + Y(0)(1-D(1)) \\

Y(D(0)) = Y(1)D(0) + Y(0)(1-D(0))

\$\$

(A3) Relevance: $E[D(1)] \neq E[D(0)]$

Additionally we impose the assumption of monotonicity and rule out defiers:

(A4) Monotonicity: $P(D(1) \geq D(0)) = 1$

First we need to show, that $\tau_{LATT}$ that is defined as $\tau_{LATT} = E[Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1]$ can be written as the ratio of the ATT of the instrument on the outcome and the ATT of the instrument on the treatment:

$$\tau_{LATT} = \frac{E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1]} {E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]}$$

We start this proof with the numerator of the second term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A2)}}{=} E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& = E[ Y(1)D(1) + Y(0)(1-D(1)) - (Y(1)D(0) + Y(0)(1-D(0))) \mid D=1] \\
&= E[Y(1)D(1) + Y(0) - Y(0)D(1) - Y(1)D(0) - Y(0) + Y(0)D(0) \mid D=1] \\
& = E[ Y(1)(D(1)-D(0)) + Y(0)(D(0)-D(1)) \mid D=1] \\
& = E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We can divide this based on four diffeerent compliance types:

1.  Always Takers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=1 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 0$
2.  Never Takers: $D(1)=0, D(0)=0 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 0$
3.  Compliers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=0 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 1$
4.  Defiers: $D(1)=0, D(0)=1 \implies D(1) - D(0) = -1$

Under our monotonicity assumption (A4) we can rule out defiers. Formally this implies that $P(D(1) - D(0) = -1 ) = P(Defiers) = P(Defiers \mid D=1) = 0$.

Also, when we condition on D = 1, never takers are not included in this group, since they never take the treatment regardless of the instrument value. Thus, when we condition on D = 1, the only compliance types that remain are always takers and compliers. Formally, $P(Never Takers | D=1) = 0$.

We can rewite our previous equation using the law of iterated expectations and conditioning on the compliance types:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Always Takers}] \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Never Takers}] \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Defiers}] \cdot P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1) \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A4)}}{=} 0 \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + 0 \cdot P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0)) \cdot 1 \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + (-1) \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& = E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& = E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] \cdot P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We have show the equivalence of:

$$
E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] = E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1)
$$

Now we can rearrange this to express $\tau_{LATT}$:

$$
E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] = \frac{E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1]}{P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1)}
$$

Now we show that the denominator can be written as $E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Always Takers}] \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Never Takers}] \cdot  \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& + E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Defiers}] \cdot P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1) \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A4)}}{=} 0 \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + 0 \cdot P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + 1 \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + (-1) \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& = P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& = P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging this back into our previous equation we get:

$$
E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] = \frac{E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1]}{E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]} \quad \blacksquare
$$

Which we wanted to show.

Now we will proceed with the identification of the LATT under the stated assumptions. We start with the numerator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A2)}}{=} E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E[\underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{Y(1) - Y(1)}} \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=1, D=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=1 \mid D=1) \\
& + E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0, D=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1) \\
& + E[Y(0) - Y(1) \mid D(1) = 0, D(0)=1, D=1] \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(D(1) = 0, D(0)=1 \mid D=1)}} \\
& + E[\underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{Y(0) - Y(0)}} \mid D(1) = 0, D(0)=0, D=1] \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(D(1) = 0, D(0)=0 \mid D=1)}} \\
& = E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0, D=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$ We can see that only the effect on compliers remains in the expression above.

If the instrument is randomly assigned and we look only at the compliers, if D=1 this is equivalent to Z=1, and if D=0 this is equivalent to Z=0. Thus we can rewrite the above expression as:

$$
E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0, D=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1) = E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0, Z=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1)
$$

Additionally if (A1) hold (Z is randomly assigned and specially independent of potential outcomes and potential treatments), we can drop the conditioning on Z=1:

$$
E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0, Z=1] = E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0] = \tau_{LATE}
$$

This is equal to the LATE effect. So, We identified the numerator as a scaled version of the LATE:

$$E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] = \tau_{LATE} \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1)$$

Now we proceed with the denominator:

We again decompose this using the law of iterated expectations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E[\underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{1 - 1}} \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=1, D=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=1 \mid D=1) \\
& + E[1 - 0 \mid D(1) = 1, D(0)=0, D=1] \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1) \\
& + E[0 - 1 \mid D(1) = 0, D(0)=1, D=1] \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(D(1) = 0, D(0)=1 \mid D=1)}} \\
& + E[\underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{0 - 0}} \mid D(1) = 0, D(0)=0, D=1] \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(D(1) = 0, D(0)=0 \mid D=1)}} \\
& = 1 \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we plug the numerator and denominator back into our expression for $\tau_{LATT}$:

$$
\tau_{LATT} = \frac{\tau_{LATE} \cdot P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1)}{P(D(1) = 1, D(0)=0 \mid D=1)} = \tau_{LATE}
$$

We have shown that under random assignment of Z that the LATT is equal to the LATE.

From the lecture we know the LATE is identified as:

$$
\tau_{LATE} = \frac{E[Y \mid Z=1] - E[Y \mid Z=0]}{E[D \mid Z=1] - E[D \mid Z=0]} = \tau_{LATT} \quad \blacksquare
$$

Therefore we have show identification of the LATT.

### 2.3 Influence function derivation

We have shown in the previous section that the LATT can be expressed as a ratio of two ATTs. As we know the Neyman orthogonal score for the ATT, we can use the chain rule to derive an influence function for the LATT.

The Neyman orthogonal score of the ATT is given by:

$$
\phi_{ATT}(W; \eta) = \underset{\textstyle=\tilde{Y}_{ATT}}{\underbrace{ \frac{W}{e}(Y - m(0,X)) - \frac{1-D}{e(1-e(X))}(Y - m(0,X))}}  - \tau_{ATT} \frac{W}{e}
$$

With $O=(D,X,Y)$, $\theta = \tau_{ATT}$, $\eta = (m(0,X), e(X), e)^1$ and the two linear score components $\phi_a = \tilde{Y}_{ATT}$ and $\phi_b = (-1)\frac{W}{e}$.

Let's tailor this score function now to the two specific ATTs we have.
